did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (YOLOv8, ResNet18, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B1, and Vision Transformers) trained on a dataset of 5000 skin lesion images to classify skin conditions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, etc.) and perform preliminary detection of skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a web-based system for skin cancer detection. It details the methodology (data collection, preprocessing, model implementation using YOLOv8, ResNet18, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B1, and Vision Transformers on a dataset of 5000 images), and reports specific results (accuracy metrics for different models and conditions). Although published in a conference proceedings (INCET), it contains original technical results and validation, fitting the definition of a research paper.

title: Web-based PoCT for Preliminary Detection of Skin Cancer Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models and Their Analysis
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based point-of-care testing system for the preliminary detection of skin cancer using five deep learning models: YOLOv8, ResNet18, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B1, and Vision Transformers. The models were trained on a dataset of 5000 labeled skin lesion images, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and benign conditions. The research evaluated model performance based on accuracy, precision, and computational efficiency to determine their suitability for real-time, remote screening. Results indicated that ResNet18 achieved the highest overall accuracy and consistency across various conditions, while YOLOv8 offered the fastest processing speed for real-time applications. The study concludes that ResNet18 is the most reliable general-purpose model for wide-spectrum skin lesion classification, supporting the feasibility of AI-driven web tools for early skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin conditions (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, nevus, actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, pigmented benign keratosis, vascular lesion, acne, normal skin) and binary cancer vs. non-cancer status.
all_task_types.details: The models (YOLOv8, ResNet18, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B1, Vision Transformers) are trained to classify skin lesions into specific discrete classes. The system predicts whether a condition is cancerous or non-cancerous and identifies the specific type of cancer or benign lesion. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Custom Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A compiled dataset of labeled skin lesion images used for training and evaluating deep learning models. The dataset includes images of various skin cancers (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and benign conditions (nevus, actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, pigmented benign keratosis, vascular lesion, acne). The source of the images is not explicitly named as a public repository (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000) in the provided text, but described as a 'dataset of skin lesion images' compiled by the authors.

use.training: Used to train five deep learning models (YOLOv8, ResNet18, InceptionV3, EfficientNet B1, and Vision Transformers) using transfer learning and fine-tuning.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance metrics including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and computational efficiency (processing time).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study compared model performance using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and computational efficiency metrics without statistical significance tests.

Custom Skin Lesion Dataset (5000 images).metric: Accuracy (%) per class across five models
Custom Skin Lesion Dataset (5000 images).notes: ResNet18 achieved the highest overall accuracy and consistency, with 100% accuracy for Acne, Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Vascular Lesions. YOLOv8 was noted for being extremely fast (~5-10 ms per image) and optimized for real-time applications. EfficientNet-B1 was slower than YOLO but faster than Inception and ViT (~10-15 ms per image). InceptionV3 was slower than EfficientNet (~20-25 ms per image). Vision Transformer had low accuracy and was slow (~30-50 ms per image). ResNet18 is identified as the most accurate and general-purpose model.

conclusions: ResNet18 is identified as the most accurate and consistent model across various skin conditions, achieving 100% accuracy for acne, dermatofibroma, squamous cell carcinoma, and vascular lesions, and high accuracy for basal cell carcinoma (99.71%), nevus (99.43%), and melanoma (74.86%).
YOLOv8 offers the fastest processing speed (~5-10 ms per image), making it optimized for real-time applications, although it has moderate accuracy compared to other models.
InceptionV3 achieved the highest accuracy for pigmented benign keratosis (93.00%) among the tested models.
Vision Transformers (ViT) demonstrated low accuracy and slower processing times (~30-50 ms per image) compared to CNN-based models.
EfficientNet-B1 showed lower accuracy than traditional CNNs like ResNet18 and InceptionV3 but was faster than them (~10-15 ms per image).
The study supports the feasibility of web-based AI-driven tools for preliminary, remote skin cancer screening to enhance accessibility and reduce the burden on healthcare systems.

A dynamic model selection architecture is proposed where an initial classification determines the input type to direct it to the highest-performing specific model for that condition, thereby improving diagnostic performance and reducing computational burden.

The system is designed to distinguish between cancerous and non-cancerous conditions and predict specific types of malignancies (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) as well as benign conditions.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom, non-public dataset of 5000 images; the specific source, collection methodology, and class distribution are not reported, raising concerns about dataset bias and reproducibility.
No external validation was performed; the models were only evaluated on the internal training/test split, limiting generalizability to diverse populations or clinical settings.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported for the performance differences between models, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the findings.
The dataset includes 'normal acne' and 'benign acne' as distinct classes, which is clinically unusual and may indicate labeling inconsistencies or a lack of standard dermatological terminology.

ResNet18 achieved 100% accuracy for Acne, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular Lesions, which suggests potential data leakage, overfitting, or an unbalanced dataset where these classes are trivially separable.

Melanoma detection accuracy was relatively low (50.11% - 74.86% across models), which is a critical limitation for a preliminary cancer detection tool, as high sensitivity is required for malignant cases.
The paper does not report standard metrics like sensitivity, specificity, or AUC-ROC in the results table, relying primarily on accuracy, which can be misleading for imbalanced datasets.

The system is designed for self-diagnosis by lay users, but the paper does not address risks of user error in image capture, lighting conditions, or the psychological impact of false positives/negatives.
The proposed 'dynamic model selection' architecture is described as future work and was not implemented or validated in this study.
The study lacks comparison with state-of-the-art public benchmarks (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000), making it difficult to contextualize the reported accuracy against established standards in the field.
